message.content:
Search the web and any local documentation for Claude Code hooks documentation. I need to understand:

1. What hook events are available (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, etc.)
2. What environment variables are passed to hook scripts
3. How hooks are configured in settings.json
4. Any best practices for hook scripts (exit codes, stdout/stderr handling, etc.)
5. Whether there are file-watch or filesystem-change events, or only prompt-based events

Check:
- Any docs/ directory in the repo
- Web search for "Claude Code hooks documentation" 
- The ~/.claude/ directory for any README or docs
- global/ directory for any documentation files

This is for planning a production-quality auto-sync system triggered by Claude Code events.

Based on the search results, here's information about Claude Code hooks documentation for PreToolUse and PostToolUse:

## Overview

PreToolUse and PostToolUse are among the events that support all four hook types (command, http, prompt, and agent).

## PreToolUse

PreToolUse runs before tool execution and can return additionalContext to provide extra context to the model. PreToolUse uses hookSpecificOutput for richer control: allow, deny, or escalate to the user, and you can also modify tool input before it runs or inject additional context for Claude.

Starting in v2.0.10, PreToolUse hooks can modify tool inputs before execution by intercepting tool calls, modifying the JSON input, and letting execution proceed with corrected parameters.

## PostToolUse

PostToolUse fires after tool completion. PostToolUse is used by UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUseFailure, Stop, SubagentStop, and ConfigChange.

## Configuration

Hooks are configured in user, project, or local project settingsÔÇöand can also be enforced via enterprise policy settings. The official documentation is available at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.

1Ôćĺ# @-linking in Claude Code
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## what it does
     4Ôćĺ- `@path/to/file` in CLAUDE.md causes that file to be collected, cached, session-wide permissions to be set and available to be read already from session start
     5Ôćĺ- acts as a "pro-active import" ÔÇö without it, i would actively have to look it up
     6Ôćĺ- `path/to/file` will in practice do a couple of things - but most importantly do this in practice: `cat /path/to/file >> mycontext`. the implementation details differ depending on where, how and what you link but that is 95% of the important part
     7Ôćĺ
     8Ôćĺ## where it works
     9Ôćĺ- inside CLAUDE.md files (global and project-level) as well as "down the directly files" six levels (at the time we last tried this out)
    10Ôćĺ- in the users messages (e.g. `@inbox.list`) it works exactly like described above - literally. it `cat /path/to/file` and appends it to your message. when small changes have happened a git diff is literally appended; between the cached version of the file available and what the users file show.
    11Ôćĺ- claudes/your messages to other instances of claude code, your subagents, your team and literally all ways you can imagine to communicate. *when you message anything it is literally a message sent from the user in the backend*
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ## how it affects permissions
    14Ôćĺ- the idea is this: a file *must* have been @-linked actively by the user somewhere directly or indirectly.
    15Ôćĺ  - when the user opens a session/path he will answer "yes reading in this directory is okay" - saying "yes all Read/@-links are ok in <path>/*"
    16Ôćĺ  - if ./CLAUDE.md @-links something outside the path it will be added as a "do you also accept these paths?"
    17Ôćĺ  - you/claude can literally write whatever stupid thing you want - the user have to approve it. i think actual sudo-priv would say no so if you do `@/root/.ssh/id_rsa` in a message to a subagent i believe i cant even give you the password. but `@/random/path/to/skogix/user/or/group/owned/file` will give me a popup asking permission
    18Ôćĺ  - if you send anything to a subagent - that subagent will trigger the same thing as if you were to do it yourself. 
    19Ôćĺ  - some special rules exists for things like gitignore and your own setting-files. (for example you should not be able to look or change .claude/settings.json without me/user first linking it to you - even when ./* is ok) 
    20Ôćĺ  - as a linux user this is fucking annoying - because dotfiles/folders follow the rules of "i'm a windows user hurr durr", making dotfiles/folders be really stupid (and that is why we use {dot/dash}-name when naming them everywhere ^^)
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ## rules for claude
    23Ôćĺ
    24Ôćĺ- **always @-link files in messages to subagents** ÔÇö a subagent cannot read files that haven't been linked. treat @-links like function arguments: if the agent needs it, link it explicitly.
    25Ôćĺ- **especially in plan mode** ÔÇö plan mode works against cached data only. if it wasn't @-linked or previously read, it doesn't exist.
    26Ôćĺ- **never assume a subagent can "just look it up"** ÔÇö glob/grep in a subagent only searches what's been cached/permitted. no @-link = no results, silently.
    27Ôćĺ- **@-linking is both context AND permission** ÔÇö you are simultaneously passing the file contents and granting access. without it the subagent is blind.
    28Ôćĺ
    29Ôćĺ## what i'm unsure about
    30Ôćĺ- whether `@` references are recursive (does an @-linked file's own @-references get followed?)
    31Ôćĺ  - six levels deep last time we checked
    32Ôćĺ- whether it works in memory files (memory.md)
    33Ôćĺ  - we havent tried - but 99% sure that it *do not* unless linked from somewhere else first
    34Ôćĺ- whether it supports globs (`@docs/*.md`) or only exact paths
    35Ôćĺ  - only `@a,@~/,@/,@./` as far as i know. (fun fact, this is why your bash tool not work when running `ls` but you literally have to run `ls .` ^^)
    36Ôćĺ- the exact resolution rules ÔÇö relative to what? the claude.md location? the working directory?
    37Ôćĺ  - relative to the file being read and when you write it your "base path/pwd" is used. (it can be set by configs to return to the same after each bash call or actually move with "cd" etc)
    38Ôćĺ- whether there's a cost/limit to how many files get @-linked
    39Ôćĺ  - there is as well as "you yourself"/claude/a instance of claude/sometimes haikuu on the backend even can decide this. *a @-link is not always included in your context* - which is why i always say it is a "please read `@/path/to/file` its really important!" but not guaranteed as in "programmed to work this way"

1Ôćĺ# RTK Transparent Command Rewriting via Claude Code Hooks
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Discovery
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺStarted by exploring RTK (Rust Token Killer) as a token optimization tool.
     6ÔćĺThe existing docs referenced v0.2.0 which said `ls` was -274% worse and `grep`
     7Ôćĺwas buggy. Testing revealed v0.22.2 was installed ÔÇö both commands now work
     8Ôćĺgreat (73% and 95% savings respectively).
     9Ôćĺ
    10Ôćĺ## The Journey: Deny Ôćĺ Discover Ôćĺ Rewrite
    11Ôćĺ
    12Ôćĺ### Phase 1: Custom deny-based hook
    13Ôćĺ
    14ÔćĺBuilt a PreToolUse hook that denied non-RTK commands and told Claude to retry
    15Ôćĺwith `rtk` prefix. Worked but cost an extra API round-trip per rewrite.
    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺ### Phase 2: RTK discover
    18Ôćĺ
    19ÔćĺRan `rtk discover` which analyzed 9 sessions (100 Bash commands) and found
    20Ôćĺ`ls -la` was the #1 missed opportunity at 31 calls. Only 6% of commands
    21Ôćĺwere using RTK.
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ### Phase 3: RTK's built-in integration
    24Ôćĺ
    25ÔćĺDiscovered `rtk init -g --auto-patch` which ships its own production-grade
    26ÔćĺClaude Code hook (`rtk-rewrite.sh`). The key insight: PreToolUse hooks can
    27Ôćĺreturn `updatedInput` in their JSON output to **transparently rewrite**
    28Ôćĺthe command before execution ÔÇö not just deny it.
    29Ôćĺ
    30Ôćĺ```json
    31Ôćĺ{
    32Ôćĺ  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    33Ôćĺ    "permissionDecision": "allow",
    34Ôćĺ    "updatedInput": { "command": "rtk git log --oneline -5" }
    35Ôćĺ  }
    36Ôćĺ}
    37Ôćĺ```
    38Ôćĺ
    39ÔćĺZero overhead. The command is modified in-place before execution. No deny,
    40Ôćĺno retry, no extra API call.
    41Ôćĺ
    42Ôćĺ### Coverage
    43Ôćĺ
    44ÔćĺRTK's hook covers 30+ command patterns across git, gh, cargo, ls, tree, find,
    45Ôćĺgrep, catÔćĺread, headÔćĺread, docker, kubectl, vitest, tsc, eslint, prettier,
    46Ôćĺplaywright, prisma, pnpm, npm, pytest, ruff, pip, go, golangci-lint, curl, wget.
    47Ôćĺ
    48Ôćĺ## Key Takeaway
    49Ôćĺ
    50ÔćĺMany CLI tools ship their own Claude Code integration. Before building custom
    51Ôćĺhooks, check if the tool has `init` or similar setup commands. RTK's hook is
    52Ôćĺfar more robust than what we built manually ÔÇö it handles env var prefixes,
    53Ôćĺheredocs, chained commands, and dozens of command patterns.
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ## Useful Commands
    56Ôćĺ
    57Ôćĺ```bash
    58Ôćĺrtk init --show       # Health check of RTK integration
    59Ôćĺrtk hook-audit        # See rewrite metrics
    60Ôćĺrtk gain              # Token savings summary
    61Ôćĺrtk discover          # Missed optimization opportunities
    62Ôćĺ```
    63Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ#!/usr/bin/env bash
     2Ôćĺset -euo pipefail
     3Ôćĺ
     4Ôćĺ# Debug hook ÔÇö logs event info to stderr and a log file
     5Ôćĺ# Receives JSON via stdin with hook_event_name and other fields
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺinput=$(cat)
     8Ôćĺevent=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.hook_event_name // "unknown"')
     9Ôćĺtool=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_name // empty')
    10Ôćĺtimestamp=$(date '+%H:%M:%S')
    11Ôćĺ
    12Ôćĺlogfile="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-.}/.hook-debug.log"
    13Ôćĺ
    14Ôćĺif [ -n "$tool" ]; then
    15Ôćĺ  line="[$timestamp] HOOK: $event (tool: $tool)"
    16Ôćĺelse
    17Ôćĺ  line="[$timestamp] HOOK: $event"
    18Ôćĺfi
    19Ôćĺ
    20Ôćĺecho "$line" >> "$logfile"
    21Ôćĺecho "$line" >&2
    22Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ<!--
     2Ôćĺname: 'System Prompt: Hooks Configuration'
     3Ôćĺdescription: System prompt for hooks configuration.  Used for above Claude Code config skill.
     4ÔćĺccVersion: 2.1.30
     5Ôćĺ-->
     6Ôćĺ## Hooks Configuration
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺHooks run commands at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle.
     9Ôćĺ
    10Ôćĺ### Hook Structure
    11Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
    12Ôćĺ{
    13Ôćĺ  "hooks": {
    14Ôćĺ    "EVENT_NAME": [
    15Ôćĺ      {
    16Ôćĺ        "matcher": "ToolName|OtherTool",
    17Ôćĺ        "hooks": [
    18Ôćĺ          {
    19Ôćĺ            "type": "command",
    20Ôćĺ            "command": "your-command-here",
    21Ôćĺ            "timeout": 60,
    22Ôćĺ            "statusMessage": "Running..."
    23Ôćĺ          }
    24Ôćĺ        ]
    25Ôćĺ      }
    26Ôćĺ    ]
    27Ôćĺ  }
    28Ôćĺ}
    29Ôćĺ\`\`\`
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ### Hook Events
    32Ôćĺ
    33Ôćĺ| Event | Matcher | Purpose |
    34Ôćĺ|-------|---------|---------|
    35Ôćĺ| PermissionRequest | Tool name | Run before permission prompt |
    36Ôćĺ| PreToolUse | Tool name | Run before tool, can block |
    37Ôćĺ| PostToolUse | Tool name | Run after successful tool |
    38Ôćĺ| PostToolUseFailure | Tool name | Run after tool fails |
    39Ôćĺ| Notification | Notification type | Run on notifications |
    40Ôćĺ| Stop | - | Run when Claude stops (including clear, resume, compact) |
    41Ôćĺ| PreCompact | "manual"/"auto" | Before compaction |
    42Ôćĺ| UserPromptSubmit | - | When user submits |
    43Ôćĺ| SessionStart | - | When session starts |
    44Ôćĺ
    45Ôćĺ**Common tool matchers:** \`Bash\`, \`Write\`, \`Edit\`, \`Read\`, \`Glob\`, \`Grep\`
    46Ôćĺ
    47Ôćĺ### Hook Types
    48Ôćĺ
    49Ôćĺ**1. Command Hook** - Runs a shell command:
    50Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
    51Ôćĺ{ "type": "command", "command": "prettier --write $FILE", "timeout": 30 }
    52Ôćĺ\`\`\`
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ**2. Prompt Hook** - Evaluates a condition with LLM:
    55Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
    56Ôćĺ{ "type": "prompt", "prompt": "Is this safe? $ARGUMENTS" }
    57Ôćĺ\`\`\`
    58ÔćĺOnly available for tool events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PermissionRequest.
    59Ôćĺ
    60Ôćĺ**3. Agent Hook** - Runs an agent with tools:
    61Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
    62Ôćĺ{ "type": "agent", "prompt": "Verify tests pass: $ARGUMENTS" }
    63Ôćĺ\`\`\`
    64ÔćĺOnly available for tool events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PermissionRequest.
    65Ôćĺ
    66Ôćĺ### Hook Input (stdin JSON)
    67Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
    68Ôćĺ{
    69Ôćĺ  "session_id": "abc123",
    70Ôćĺ  "tool_name": "Write",
    71Ôćĺ  "tool_input": { "file_path": "/path/to/file.txt", "content": "..." },
    72Ôćĺ  "tool_response": { "success": true }  // PostToolUse only
    73Ôćĺ}
    74Ôćĺ\`\`\`
    75Ôćĺ
    76Ôćĺ### Hook JSON Output
    77Ôćĺ
    78ÔćĺHooks can return JSON to control behavior:
    79Ôćĺ
    80Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
    81Ôćĺ{
    82Ôćĺ  "systemMessage": "Warning shown to user in UI",
    83Ôćĺ  "continue": false,
    84Ôćĺ  "stopReason": "Message shown when blocking",
    85Ôćĺ  "suppressOutput": false,
    86Ôćĺ  "decision": "block",
    87Ôćĺ  "reason": "Explanation for decision",
    88Ôćĺ  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    89Ôćĺ    "hookEventName": "PostToolUse",
    90Ôćĺ    "additionalContext": "Context injected back to model"
    91Ôćĺ  }
    92Ôćĺ}
    93Ôćĺ\`\`\`
    94Ôćĺ
    95Ôćĺ**Fields:**
    96Ôćĺ- \`systemMessage\` - Display a message to the user (all hooks)
    97Ôćĺ- \`continue\` - Set to \`false\` to block/stop (default: true)
    98Ôćĺ- \`stopReason\` - Message shown when \`continue\` is false
    99Ôćĺ- \`suppressOutput\` - Hide stdout from transcript (default: false)
   100Ôćĺ- \`decision\` - "block" for PostToolUse/Stop/UserPromptSubmit hooks (deprecated for PreToolUse, use hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecision instead)
   101Ôćĺ- \`reason\` - Explanation for decision
   102Ôćĺ- \`hookSpecificOutput\` - Event-specific output (must include \`hookEventName\`):
   103Ôćĺ  - \`additionalContext\` - Text injected into model context
   104Ôćĺ  - \`permissionDecision\` - "allow", "deny", or "ask" (PreToolUse only)
   105Ôćĺ  - \`permissionDecisionReason\` - Reason for the permission decision (PreToolUse only)
   106Ôćĺ  - \`updatedInput\` - Modified tool input (PreToolUse only)
   107Ôćĺ
   108Ôćĺ### Common Patterns
   109Ôćĺ
   110Ôćĺ**Auto-format after writes:**
   111Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
   112Ôćĺ{
   113Ôćĺ  "hooks": {
   114Ôćĺ    "PostToolUse": [{
   115Ôćĺ      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
   116Ôćĺ      "hooks": [{
   117Ôćĺ        "type": "command",
   118Ôćĺ        "command": "jq -r '.tool_response.filePath // .tool_input.file_path' | xargs prettier --write 2>/dev/null || true"
   119Ôćĺ      }]
   120Ôćĺ    }]
   121Ôćĺ  }
   122Ôćĺ}
   123Ôćĺ\`\`\`
   124Ôćĺ
   125Ôćĺ**Log all bash commands:**
   126Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
   127Ôćĺ{
   128Ôćĺ  "hooks": {
   129Ôćĺ    "PreToolUse": [{
   130Ôćĺ      "matcher": "Bash",
   131Ôćĺ      "hooks": [{
   132Ôćĺ        "type": "command",
   133Ôćĺ        "command": "jq -r '.tool_input.command' >> ~/.claude/bash-log.txt"
   134Ôćĺ      }]
   135Ôćĺ    }]
   136Ôćĺ  }
   137Ôćĺ}
   138Ôćĺ\`\`\`
   139Ôćĺ
   140Ôćĺ**Stop hook that displays message to user:**
   141Ôćĺ
   142ÔćĺCommand must output JSON with \`systemMessage\` field:
   143Ôćĺ\`\`\`bash
   144Ôćĺ# Example command that outputs: {"systemMessage": "Session complete!"}
   145Ôćĺecho '{"systemMessage": "Session complete!"}'
   146Ôćĺ\`\`\`
   147Ôćĺ
   148Ôćĺ**Run tests after code changes:**
   149Ôćĺ\`\`\`json
   150Ôćĺ{
   151Ôćĺ  "hooks": {
   152Ôćĺ    "PostToolUse": [{
   153Ôćĺ      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
   154Ôćĺ      "hooks": [{
   155Ôćĺ        "type": "command",
   156Ôćĺ        "command": "jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // .tool_response.filePath' | grep -E '\\\\.(ts|js)$' && npm test || true"
   157Ôćĺ      }]
   158Ôćĺ    }]
   159Ôćĺ  }
   160Ôćĺ}
   161Ôćĺ\`\`\`
   162Ôćĺ

# Hooks reference

> Reference for Claude Code hook events, configuration schema, JSON input/output formats, exit codes, async hooks, HTTP hooks, prompt hooks, and MCP tool hooks.

<Tip>

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Use this reference to look up event schemas, configuration options, JSON input/output formats, and advanced features like async hooks, HTTP hooks, and MCP tool hooks. If you're setting up hooks for the first time, start with the [guide](/en/hooks-guide) instead.

## Hook lifecycle

Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. For command hooks, input arrives on stdin. For HTTP hooks, it arrives as the POST request body. Your handler can then inspect the input, take action, and optionally return a decision. Some events fire once per session, while others fire repeatedly inside the agentic loop:

If you're setting up hooks for the first time, start with the [guide](/en/hooks-guide) instead. 14Ôćĺ 14Ôćĺ 15Ôćĺ 15Ôćĺ## Hook lifecycle 16Ôćĺ 16Ôćĺ 17Ôćĺ 17ÔćĺHooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. For command hooks, input arrives on stdin. For HTTP hooks, it arrives as the POST request body. Your handler can then inspect the input, take action, and optionally return a decision. Some events fire once per session, while others fire repeatedly inside the agentic loop: 18Ôćĺ 18Ôćĺ 19Ôćĺ 19Ôćĺ
20Ôćĺ 20Ôćĺ 21Ôćĺ 21Ôćĺ 22Ôćĺ 22Ôćĺ 23Ôćĺ 23Ôćĺ
24Ôćĺ 24Ôćĺ 25Ôćĺ 25ÔćĺThe table below summarizes when each event fires. The [Hook events](#hook-events) section documents the full input schema and decision control options for each one. 26Ôćĺ 26Ôćĺ 27Ôćĺ 27Ôćĺ| Event | When it fires | 28Ôćĺ 28Ôćĺ| :------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 29Ôćĺ 29Ôćĺ| `SessionStart` | When a session begins or resumes | 30Ôćĺ 30Ôćĺ| `UserPromptSubmit` | When you submit a prompt, before Claude processes it | 31Ôćĺ 31Ôćĺ| `PreToolUse` | Before a tool call executes. Can block it | 32Ôćĺ 32Ôćĺ| `PermissionRequest` | When a permission dialog appears | 33Ôćĺ 33Ôćĺ| `PostToolUse` | After a tool call succeeds | 34Ôćĺ 34Ôćĺ| `PostToolUseFailure` | After a tool call fails | 35Ôćĺ 35Ôćĺ| `Notification` | When Claude Code sends a notification | 36Ôćĺ 36Ôćĺ| `SubagentStart` | When a subagent is spawned | 37Ôćĺ 37Ôćĺ| `SubagentStop` | When a subagent finishes | 38Ôćĺ 38Ôćĺ| `Stop` | When Claude finishes responding | 39Ôćĺ 39Ôćĺ| `TeammateIdle` | When an [agent team](/en/agent-teams) teammate is about to go idle | 40Ôćĺ 40Ôćĺ| `TaskCompleted` | When a task is being marked as completed | 41Ôćĺ 41Ôćĺ| `ConfigChange` | When a configuration file changes during a session | 42Ôćĺ 42Ôćĺ| `WorktreeCreate` | When a worktree is being created via `--worktree` or `isolation: "worktree"`. Replaces default git behavior | 43Ôćĺ 43Ôćĺ| `WorktreeRemove` | When a worktree is being removed, either at session exit or when a subagent finishes | 44Ôćĺ 44Ôćĺ| `PreCompact` | Before context compaction | 45Ôćĺ 45Ôćĺ| `SessionEnd` | When a session terminates | 46Ôćĺ 46Ôćĺ 47Ôćĺ 47Ôćĺ### How a hook resolves 48Ôćĺ 48Ôćĺ 49Ôćĺ 49ÔćĺTo see how these pieces fit together, consider this `PreToolUse` hook that blocks destructive shell commands. The hook runs `block-rm.sh` before every Bash tool call: 50Ôćĺ 50Ôćĺ 51Ôćĺ 51Ôćĺ```json theme={null} 52Ôćĺ 52Ôćĺ{ 53Ôćĺ 53Ôćĺ "hooks": { 54Ôćĺ 54Ôćĺ "PreToolUse": [ 55Ôćĺ 55Ôćĺ { 56Ôćĺ 56Ôćĺ "matcher": "Bash", 57Ôćĺ 57Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 58Ôćĺ 58Ôćĺ { 59Ôćĺ 59Ôćĺ "type": "command", 60Ôćĺ 60Ôćĺ "command": ".claude/hooks/block-rm.sh" 61Ôćĺ 61Ôćĺ } 62Ôćĺ 62Ôćĺ ] 63Ôćĺ 63Ôćĺ } 64Ôćĺ 64Ôćĺ ] 65Ôćĺ 65Ôćĺ } 66Ôćĺ 66Ôćĺ} 67Ôćĺ 67Ôćĺ``` 68Ôćĺ 68Ôćĺ 69Ôćĺ 69ÔćĺThe script reads the JSON input from stdin, extracts the command, and returns a `permissionDecision` of `"deny"` if it contains `rm -rf`: 70Ôćĺ 70Ôćĺ 71Ôćĺ 71Ôćĺ```bash theme={null} 72Ôćĺ 72Ôćĺ#!/bin/bash 73Ôćĺ 73Ôćĺ# .claude/hooks/block-rm.sh 74Ôćĺ 74ÔćĺCOMMAND=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command') 75Ôćĺ 75Ôćĺ 76Ôćĺ 76Ôćĺif echo "$COMMAND" | grep -q 'rm -rf'; then 77Ôćĺ 77Ôćĺ jq -n '{ 78Ôćĺ 78Ôćĺ hookSpecificOutput: { 79Ôćĺ 79Ôćĺ hookEventName: "PreToolUse", 80Ôćĺ 80Ôćĺ permissionDecision: "deny", 81Ôćĺ 81Ôćĺ permissionDecisionReason: "Destructive command blocked by hook" 82Ôćĺ 82Ôćĺ } 83Ôćĺ 83Ôćĺ }' 84Ôćĺ 84Ôćĺelse 85Ôćĺ 85Ôćĺ exit 0 # allow the command 86Ôćĺ 86Ôćĺfi 87Ôćĺ 87Ôćĺ``` 88Ôćĺ 88Ôćĺ 89Ôćĺ 89ÔćĺNow suppose Claude Code decides to run `Bash "rm -rf /tmp/build"`. Here's what happens: 90Ôćĺ 90Ôćĺ 91Ôćĺ 91Ôćĺ 92Ôćĺ 92Ôćĺ 93Ôćĺ 93Ôćĺ 94Ôćĺ 94Ôćĺ 95Ôćĺ 95Ôćĺ 96Ôćĺ 96Ôćĺ 97Ôćĺ 97Ôćĺ The `PreToolUse` event fires. Claude Code sends the tool input as JSON on stdin to the hook: 98Ôćĺ 98Ôćĺ 99Ôćĺ 99Ôćĺ ```json theme={null} 100Ôćĺ 100Ôćĺ { "tool_name": "Bash", "tool_input": { "command": "rm -rf /tmp/build" }, ... } 101Ôćĺ 101Ôćĺ ``` 102Ôćĺ 102Ôćĺ 103Ôćĺ 103Ôćĺ 104Ôćĺ 104Ôćĺ 105Ôćĺ 105Ôćĺ The matcher `"Bash"` matches the tool name, so `block-rm.sh` runs. If you omit the matcher or use `"*"`, the hook runs on every occurrence of the event. Hooks only skip when a matcher is defined and doesn't match. 106Ôćĺ 106Ôćĺ 107Ôćĺ 107Ôćĺ 108Ôćĺ 108Ôćĺ 109Ôćĺ 109Ôćĺ The script extracts `"rm -rf /tmp/build"` from the input and finds `rm -rf`, so it prints a decision to stdout: 110Ôćĺ 110Ôćĺ 111Ôćĺ 111Ôćĺ ```json theme={null} 112Ôćĺ 112Ôćĺ { 113Ôćĺ 113Ôćĺ "hookSpecificOutput": { 114Ôćĺ 114Ôćĺ "hookEventName": "PreToolUse", 115Ôćĺ 115Ôćĺ "permissionDecision": "deny", 116Ôćĺ 116Ôćĺ "permissionDecisionReason": "Destructive command blocked by hook" 117Ôćĺ 117Ôćĺ } 118Ôćĺ 118Ôćĺ } 119Ôćĺ 119Ôćĺ ``` 120Ôćĺ 120Ôćĺ 121Ôćĺ 121Ôćĺ If the command had been safe (like `npm test`), the script would hit `exit 0` instead, which tells Claude Code to allow the tool call with no further action. 122Ôćĺ 122Ôćĺ 123Ôćĺ 123Ôćĺ 124Ôćĺ 124Ôćĺ 125Ôćĺ 125Ôćĺ Claude Code reads the JSON decision, blocks the tool call, and shows Claude the reason. 126Ôćĺ 126Ôćĺ 127Ôćĺ 127Ôćĺ 128Ôćĺ 128Ôćĺ 129Ôćĺ 129ÔćĺThe [Configuration](#configuration) section below documents the full schema, and each [hook event](#hook-events) section documents what input your command receives and what output it can return. 130Ôćĺ 130Ôćĺ 131Ôćĺ 131Ôćĺ## Configuration 132Ôćĺ 132Ôćĺ 133Ôćĺ 133ÔćĺHooks are defined in JSON settings files. The configuration has three levels of nesting: 134Ôćĺ 134Ôćĺ 135Ôćĺ 135Ôćĺ1. Choose a [hook event](#hook-events) to respond to, like `PreToolUse` or `Stop` 136Ôćĺ 136Ôćĺ2. Add a [matcher group](#matcher-patterns) to filter when it fires, like "only for the Bash tool" 137Ôćĺ 137Ôćĺ3. Define one or more [hook handlers](#hook-handler-fields) to run when matched 138Ôćĺ 138Ôćĺ 139Ôćĺ 139ÔćĺSee [How a hook resolves](#how-a-hook-resolves) above for a complete walkthrough with an annotated example. 140Ôćĺ 140Ôćĺ 141Ôćĺ 141Ôćĺ 142Ôćĺ 142Ôćĺ This page uses specific terms for each level: **hook event** for the lifecycle point, **matcher group** for the filter, and **hook handler** for the shell command, HTTP endpoint, prompt, or agent that runs. "Hook" on its own refers to the general feature. 143Ôćĺ 143Ôćĺ 144Ôćĺ 144Ôćĺ 145Ôćĺ 145Ôćĺ### Hook locations 146Ôćĺ 146Ôćĺ 147Ôćĺ 147ÔćĺWhere you define a hook determines its scope: 148Ôćĺ 148Ôćĺ 149Ôćĺ 149Ôćĺ| Location | Scope | Shareable | 150Ôćĺ 150Ôćĺ| :--------------------------------------------------------- | :---------------------------- | :--------------------------------- | 151Ôćĺ 151Ôćĺ| `~/.claude/settings.json` | All your projects | No, local to your machine | 152Ôćĺ 152Ôćĺ| `.claude/settings.json` | Single project | Yes, can be committed to the repo | 153Ôćĺ 153Ôćĺ| `.claude/settings.local.json` | Single project | No, gitignored | 154Ôćĺ 154Ôćĺ| Managed policy settings | Organization-wide | Yes, admin-controlled | 155Ôćĺ 155Ôćĺ| [Plugin](/en/plugins) `hooks/hooks.json` | When plugin is enabled | Yes, bundled with the plugin | 156Ôćĺ 156Ôćĺ| [Skill](/en/skills) or [agent](/en/sub-agents) frontmatter | While the component is active | Yes, defined in the component file | 157Ôćĺ 157Ôćĺ 158Ôćĺ 158ÔćĺFor details on settings file resolution, see [settings](/en/settings). Enterprise administrators can use `allowManagedHooksOnly` to block user, project, and plugin hooks. See [Hook configuration](/en/settings#hook-configuration). 159Ôćĺ 159Ôćĺ 160Ôćĺ 160Ôćĺ### Matcher patterns 161Ôćĺ 161Ôćĺ 162Ôćĺ 162ÔćĺThe `matcher` field is a regex string that filters when hooks fire. Use `"*"`, `""`, or omit `matcher` entirely to match all occurrences. Each event type matches on a different field: 163Ôćĺ 163Ôćĺ 164Ôćĺ 164Ôćĺ| Event | What the matcher filters | Example matcher values | 165Ôćĺ 165Ôćĺ| :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 166Ôćĺ 166Ôćĺ| `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `PermissionRequest` | tool name | `Bash`, `Edit\|Write`, `mcp__.*` | 167Ôćĺ 167Ôćĺ| `SessionStart` | how the session started | `startup`, `resume`, `clear`, `compact` | 168Ôćĺ 168Ôćĺ| `SessionEnd` | why the session ended | `clear`, `logout`, `prompt_input_exit`, `bypass_permissions_disabled`, `other` | 169Ôćĺ 169Ôćĺ| `Notification` | notification type | `permission_prompt`, `idle_prompt`, `auth_success`, `elicitation_dialog` | 170Ôćĺ 170Ôćĺ| `SubagentStart` | agent type | `Bash`, `Explore`, `Plan`, or custom agent names | 171Ôćĺ 171Ôćĺ| `PreCompact` | what triggered compaction | `manual`, `auto` | 172Ôćĺ 172Ôćĺ| `SubagentStop` | agent type | same values as `SubagentStart` | 173Ôćĺ 173Ôćĺ| `ConfigChange` | configuration source | `user_settings`, `project_settings`, `local_settings`, `policy_settings`, `skills` | 174Ôćĺ 174Ôćĺ| `UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `TeammateIdle`, `TaskCompleted`, `WorktreeCreate`, `WorktreeRemove` | no matcher support | always fires on every occurrence | 175Ôćĺ 175Ôćĺ 176Ôćĺ 176ÔćĺThe matcher is a regex, so `Edit|Write` matches either tool and `Notebook.*` matches any tool starting with Notebook. The matcher runs against a field from the [JSON input](#hook-input-and-output) that Claude Code sends to your hook on stdin. For tool events, that field is `tool_name`. Each [hook event](#hook-events) section lists the full set of matcher values and the input schema for that event. 177Ôćĺ 177Ôćĺ 178Ôćĺ 178ÔćĺThis example runs a linting script only when Claude writes or edits a file: 179Ôćĺ 179Ôćĺ 180Ôćĺ 180Ôćĺ```json theme={null} 181Ôćĺ 181Ôćĺ{ 182Ôćĺ 182Ôćĺ "hooks": { 183Ôćĺ 183Ôćĺ "PostToolUse": [ 184Ôćĺ 184Ôćĺ { 185Ôćĺ 185Ôćĺ "matcher": "Edit|Write", 186Ôćĺ 186Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 187Ôćĺ 187Ôćĺ { 188Ôćĺ 188Ôćĺ "type": "command", 189Ôćĺ 189Ôćĺ "command": "/path/to/lint-check.sh" 190Ôćĺ 190Ôćĺ } 191Ôćĺ 191Ôćĺ ] 192Ôćĺ 192Ôćĺ } 193Ôćĺ 193Ôćĺ ] 194Ôćĺ 194Ôćĺ } 195Ôćĺ 195Ôćĺ} 196Ôćĺ 196Ôćĺ``` 197Ôćĺ 197Ôćĺ 198Ôćĺ 198Ôćĺ`UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `TeammateIdle`, `TaskCompleted`, `WorktreeCreate`, and `WorktreeRemove` don't support matchers and always fire on every occurrence. If you add a `matcher` field to these events, it is silently ignored. 199Ôćĺ 199Ôćĺ 200Ôćĺ 200Ôćĺ#### Match MCP tools 201Ôćĺ 201Ôćĺ 202Ôćĺ 202Ôćĺ[MCP](/en/mcp) server tools appear as regular tools in tool events (`PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `PermissionRequest`), so you can match them the same way you match any other tool name. 203Ôćĺ 203Ôćĺ 204Ôćĺ 204ÔćĺMCP tools follow the naming pattern `mcp____`, for example: 205Ôćĺ 205Ôćĺ 206Ôćĺ 206Ôćĺ* `mcp__memory__create_entities`: Memory server's create entities tool 207Ôćĺ 207Ôćĺ* `mcp__filesystem__read_file`: Filesystem server's read file tool 208Ôćĺ 208Ôćĺ* `mcp__github__search_repositories`: GitHub server's search tool 209Ôćĺ 209Ôćĺ 210Ôćĺ 210ÔćĺUse regex patterns to target specific MCP tools or groups of tools: 211Ôćĺ 211Ôćĺ 212Ôćĺ 212Ôćĺ* `mcp__memory__.*` matches all tools from the `memory` server 213Ôćĺ 213Ôćĺ* `mcp__.*__write.*` matches any tool containing "write" from any server 214Ôćĺ 214Ôćĺ 215Ôćĺ 215ÔćĺThis example logs all memory server operations and validates write operations from any MCP server: 216Ôćĺ 216Ôćĺ 217Ôćĺ 217Ôćĺ```json theme={null} 218Ôćĺ 218Ôćĺ{ 219Ôćĺ 219Ôćĺ "hooks": { 220Ôćĺ 220Ôćĺ "PreToolUse": [ 221Ôćĺ 221Ôćĺ { 222Ôćĺ 222Ôćĺ "matcher": "mcp__memory__.*", 223Ôćĺ 223Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 224Ôćĺ 224Ôćĺ { 225Ôćĺ 225Ôćĺ "type": "command", 226Ôćĺ 226Ôćĺ "command": "echo 'Memory operation initiated' >> ~/mcp-operations.log" 227Ôćĺ 227Ôćĺ } 228Ôćĺ 228Ôćĺ ] 229Ôćĺ 229Ôćĺ }, 230Ôćĺ 230Ôćĺ { 231Ôćĺ 231Ôćĺ "matcher": "mcp__.*__write.*", 232Ôćĺ 232Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 233Ôćĺ 233Ôćĺ { 234Ôćĺ 234Ôćĺ "type": "command", 235Ôćĺ 235Ôćĺ "command": "/home/user/scripts/validate-mcp-write.py" 236Ôćĺ 236Ôćĺ } 237Ôćĺ 237Ôćĺ ] 238Ôćĺ 238Ôćĺ } 239Ôćĺ 239Ôćĺ ] 240Ôćĺ 240Ôćĺ } 241Ôćĺ 241Ôćĺ} 242Ôćĺ 242Ôćĺ``` 243Ôćĺ 243Ôćĺ 244Ôćĺ 244Ôćĺ### Hook handler fields 245Ôćĺ 245Ôćĺ 246Ôćĺ 246ÔćĺEach object in the inner `hooks` array is a hook handler: the shell command, HTTP endpoint, LLM prompt, or agent that runs when the matcher matches. There are four types: 247Ôćĺ 247Ôćĺ 248Ôćĺ 248Ôćĺ* **[Command hooks](#command-hook-fields)** (`type: "command"`): run a shell command. Your script receives the event's [JSON input](#hook-input-and-output) on stdin and communicates results back through exit codes and stdout. 249Ôćĺ 249Ôćĺ* **[HTTP hooks](#http-hook-fields)** (`type: "http"`): send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL. The endpoint communicates results back through the response body using the same [JSON output format](#json-output) as command hooks. 250Ôćĺ 250Ôćĺ* **[Prompt hooks](#prompt-and-agent-hook-fields)** (`type: "prompt"`): send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation. The model returns a yes/no decision as JSON. See [Prompt-based hooks](#prompt-based-hooks). 251Ôćĺ 251Ôćĺ* **[Agent hooks](#prompt-and-agent-hook-fields)** (`type: "agent"`): spawn a subagent that can use tools like Read, Grep, and Glob to verify conditions before returning a decision. See [Agent-based hooks](#agent-based-hooks). 252Ôćĺ 252Ôćĺ 253Ôćĺ 253Ôćĺ#### Common fields 254Ôćĺ 254Ôćĺ 255Ôćĺ 255ÔćĺThese fields apply to all hook types: 256Ôćĺ 256Ôćĺ 257Ôćĺ 257Ôćĺ| Field | Required | Description | 258Ôćĺ 258Ôćĺ| :-------------- | :------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 259Ôćĺ 259Ôćĺ| `type` | yes | `"command"`, `"http"`, `"prompt"`, or `"agent"` | 260Ôćĺ 260Ôćĺ| `timeout` | no | Seconds before canceling. Defaults: 600 for command, 30 for prompt, 60 for agent | 261Ôćĺ 261Ôćĺ| `statusMessage` | no | Custom spinner message displayed while the hook runs | 262Ôćĺ 262Ôćĺ| `once` | no | If `true`, runs only once per session then is removed. Skills only, not agents. See [Hooks in skills and agents](#hooks-in-skills-and-agents) | 263Ôćĺ 263Ôćĺ 264Ôćĺ 264Ôćĺ#### Command hook fields 265Ôćĺ 265Ôćĺ 266Ôćĺ 266ÔćĺIn addition to the [common fields](#common-fields), command hooks accept these fields: 267Ôćĺ 267Ôćĺ 268Ôćĺ 268Ôćĺ| Field | Required | Description | 269Ôćĺ 269Ôćĺ| :-------- | :------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | 270Ôćĺ 270Ôćĺ| `command` | yes | Shell command to execute | 271Ôćĺ 271Ôćĺ| `async` | no | If `true`, runs in the background without blocking. See [Run hooks in the background](#run-hooks-in-the-background) | 272Ôćĺ 272Ôćĺ 273Ôćĺ 273Ôćĺ#### HTTP hook fields 274Ôćĺ 274Ôćĺ 275Ôćĺ 275ÔćĺIn addition to the [common fields](#common-fields), HTTP hooks accept these fields: 276Ôćĺ 276Ôćĺ 277Ôćĺ 277Ôćĺ| Field | Required | Description | 278Ôćĺ 278Ôćĺ| :--------------- | :------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 279Ôćĺ 279Ôćĺ| `url` | yes | URL to send the POST request to | 280Ôćĺ 280Ôćĺ| `headers` | no | Additional HTTP headers as key-value pairs. Values support environment variable interpolation using `$VAR_NAME` or `${VAR_NAME}` syntax. Only variables listed in `allowedEnvVars` are resolved | 281Ôćĺ 281Ôćĺ| `allowedEnvVars` | no | List of environment variable names that may be interpolated into header values. References to unlisted variables are replaced with empty strings. Required for any env var interpolation to work | 282Ôćĺ 282Ôćĺ 283Ôćĺ 283ÔćĺClaude Code sends the hook's [JSON input](#hook-input-and-output) as the POST request body with `Content-Type: application/json`. The response body uses the same [JSON output format](#json-output) as command hooks. 284Ôćĺ 284Ôćĺ 285Ôćĺ 285ÔćĺError handling differs from command hooks: non-2xx responses, connection failures, and timeouts all produce non-blocking errors that allow execution to continue. To block a tool call or deny a permission, return a 2xx response with a JSON body containing `decision: "block"` or a `hookSpecificOutput` with `permissionDecision: "deny"`. 286Ôćĺ 286Ôćĺ 287Ôćĺ 287ÔćĺThis example sends `PreToolUse` events to a local validation service, authenticating with a token from the `MY_TOKEN` environment variable: 288Ôćĺ 288Ôćĺ 289Ôćĺ 289Ôćĺ```json theme={null} 290Ôćĺ 290Ôćĺ{ 291Ôćĺ 291Ôćĺ "hooks": { 292Ôćĺ 292Ôćĺ "PreToolUse": [ 293Ôćĺ 293Ôćĺ { 294Ôćĺ 294Ôćĺ "matcher": "Bash", 295Ôćĺ 295Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 296Ôćĺ 296Ôćĺ { 297Ôćĺ 297Ôćĺ "type": "http", 298Ôćĺ 298Ôćĺ "url": "http://localhost:8080/hooks/pre-tool-use", 299Ôćĺ 299Ôćĺ "timeout": 30, 300Ôćĺ 300Ôćĺ "headers": { 301Ôćĺ 301Ôćĺ "Authorization": "Bearer $MY_TOKEN" 302Ôćĺ 302Ôćĺ }, 303Ôćĺ 303Ôćĺ "allowedEnvVars": ["MY_TOKEN"] 304Ôćĺ 304Ôćĺ } 305Ôćĺ 305Ôćĺ ] 306Ôćĺ 306Ôćĺ } 307Ôćĺ 307Ôćĺ ] 308Ôćĺ 308Ôćĺ } 309Ôćĺ 309Ôćĺ} 310Ôćĺ 310Ôćĺ``` 311Ôćĺ 311Ôćĺ 312Ôćĺ 312Ôćĺ 313Ôćĺ 313Ôćĺ HTTP hooks must be configured by editing settings JSON directly. The `/hooks` interactive menu only supports adding command hooks. 314Ôćĺ 314Ôćĺ 315Ôćĺ 315Ôćĺ 316Ôćĺ 316Ôćĺ#### Prompt and agent hook fields 317Ôćĺ 317Ôćĺ 318Ôćĺ 318ÔćĺIn addition to the [common fields](#common-fields), prompt and agent hooks accept these fields: 319Ôćĺ 319Ôćĺ 320Ôćĺ 320Ôćĺ| Field | Required | Description | 321Ôćĺ 321Ôćĺ| :------- | :------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | 322Ôćĺ 322Ôćĺ| `prompt` | yes | Prompt text to send to the model. Use `$ARGUMENTS` as a placeholder for the hook input JSON | 323Ôćĺ 323Ôćĺ| `model` | no | Model to use for evaluation. Defaults to a fast model | 324Ôćĺ 324Ôćĺ 325Ôćĺ 325ÔćĺAll matching hooks run in parallel, and identical handlers are deduplicated automatically. Command hooks are deduplicated by command string, and HTTP hooks are deduplicated by URL. Handlers run in the current directory with Claude Code's environment. The `$CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE` environment variable is set to `"true"` in remote web environments and not set in the local CLI. 326Ôćĺ 326Ôćĺ 327Ôćĺ 327Ôćĺ### Reference scripts by path 328Ôćĺ 328Ôćĺ 329Ôćĺ 329ÔćĺUse environment variables to reference hook scripts relative to the project or plugin root, regardless of the working directory when the hook runs: 330Ôćĺ 330Ôćĺ 331Ôćĺ 331Ôćĺ* `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR`: the project root. Wrap in quotes to handle paths with spaces. 332Ôćĺ 332Ôćĺ* `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`: the plugin's root directory, for scripts bundled with a [plugin](/en/plugins). 333Ôćĺ 333Ôćĺ 334Ôćĺ 334Ôćĺ 335Ôćĺ 335Ôćĺ 336Ôćĺ 336Ôćĺ This example uses `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` to run a style checker from the project's `.claude/hooks/` directory after any `Write` or `Edit` tool call: 337Ôćĺ 337Ôćĺ 338Ôćĺ 338Ôćĺ ```json theme={null} 339Ôćĺ 339Ôćĺ { 340Ôćĺ 340Ôćĺ "hooks": { 341Ôćĺ 341Ôćĺ "PostToolUse": [ 342Ôćĺ 342Ôćĺ { 343Ôćĺ 343Ôćĺ "matcher": "Write|Edit", 344Ôćĺ 344Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 345Ôćĺ 345Ôćĺ { 346Ôćĺ 346Ôćĺ "type": "command", 347Ôćĺ 347Ôćĺ "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/check-style.sh" 348Ôćĺ 348Ôćĺ } 349Ôćĺ 349Ôćĺ ] 350Ôćĺ 350Ôćĺ } 351Ôćĺ 351Ôćĺ ] 352Ôćĺ 352Ôćĺ } 353Ôćĺ 353Ôćĺ } 354Ôćĺ 354Ôćĺ ``` 355Ôćĺ 355Ôćĺ 356Ôćĺ 356Ôćĺ 357Ôćĺ 357Ôćĺ 358Ôćĺ 358Ôćĺ Define plugin hooks in `hooks/hooks.json` with an optional top-level `description` field. When a plugin is enabled, its hooks merge with your user and project hooks. 359Ôćĺ 359Ôćĺ 360Ôćĺ 360Ôćĺ This example runs a formatting script bundled with the plugin: 361Ôćĺ 361Ôćĺ 362Ôćĺ 362Ôćĺ ```json theme={null} 363Ôćĺ 363Ôćĺ { 364Ôćĺ 364Ôćĺ "description": "Automatic code formatting", 365Ôćĺ 365Ôćĺ "hooks": { 366Ôćĺ 366Ôćĺ "PostToolUse": [ 367Ôćĺ 367Ôćĺ { 368Ôćĺ 368Ôćĺ "matcher": "Write|Edit", 369Ôćĺ 369Ôćĺ "hooks": [ 370Ôćĺ 370Ôćĺ { 371Ôćĺ 371Ôćĺ "type": "command", 372Ôćĺ 372Ôćĺ "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/format.sh", 373Ôćĺ 373Ôćĺ "timeout": 30 374Ôćĺ 374Ôćĺ } 375Ôćĺ 375Ôćĺ ] 376Ôćĺ 376Ôćĺ } 377Ôćĺ 377Ôćĺ ] 378Ôćĺ 378Ôćĺ } 379Ôćĺ 379Ôćĺ } 380Ôćĺ 380Ôćĺ ``` 381Ôćĺ 381Ôćĺ 382Ôćĺ 382Ôćĺ See the [plugin components reference](/en/plugins-reference#hooks) for details on creating plugin hooks. 383Ôćĺ 383Ôćĺ 384Ôćĺ 384Ôćĺ 385Ôćĺ 385Ôćĺ 386Ôćĺ 386Ôćĺ### Hooks in skills and agents 387Ôćĺ 387Ôćĺ 388Ôćĺ 388ÔćĺIn addition to settings files and plugins, hooks can be defined directly in [skills](/en/skills) and [subagents](/en/sub-agents) using frontmatter. These hooks are scoped to the component's lifecycle and only run when that component is active. 389Ôćĺ 389Ôćĺ 390Ôćĺ 390ÔćĺAll hook events are supported. For subagents, `Stop` hooks are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` since that is the event that fires when a subagent completes. 391Ôćĺ 391Ôćĺ 392Ôćĺ 392ÔćĺHooks use the same configuration format as settings-based hooks but are scoped to the component's lifetime and cleaned up when it finishes. 393Ôćĺ 393Ôćĺ 394Ôćĺ 394ÔćĺThis skill defines a `PreToolUse` hook that runs a security validation script before each `Bash` command: 395Ôćĺ 395Ôćĺ 396Ôćĺ 396Ôćĺ```yaml theme={null} 397Ôćĺ 397Ôćĺ--- 398Ôćĺ 398Ôćĺname: secure-operations 399Ôćĺ 399Ôćĺdescription: Perform operations with security checks 400Ôćĺ 400Ôćĺhooks: 401Ôćĺ 401Ôćĺ PreToolUse: 402Ôćĺ 402Ôćĺ - matcher: "Bash" 403Ôćĺ 403Ôćĺ hooks: 404Ôćĺ 404Ôćĺ - type: command 405Ôćĺ 405Ôćĺ command: "./scripts/security-check.sh" 406Ôćĺ 406Ôćĺ--- 407Ôćĺ 407Ôćĺ``` 408Ôćĺ 408Ôćĺ 409Ôćĺ 409ÔćĺAgents use the same format in their YAML frontmatter. 410Ôćĺ 410Ôćĺ 411Ôćĺ 411Ôćĺ### The `/hooks` menu 412Ôćĺ 412Ôćĺ 413Ôćĺ 413ÔćĺType `/hooks` in Claude Code to open the interactive hooks manager, where you can view, add, and delete hooks without editing settings files directly. For a step-by-step walkthrough, see [Set up your first hook](/en/hooks-guide#set-up-your-first-hook) in the guide. 414Ôćĺ 414Ôćĺ 415Ôćĺ 415ÔćĺEach hook in the menu is labeled with a bracket prefix indicating its source: 416Ôćĺ 416Ôćĺ 417Ôćĺ 417Ôćĺ* `[User]`: from `~/.claude/settings.json` 418Ôćĺ 418Ôćĺ* `[Project]`: from `.claude/settings.json` 419Ôćĺ 419Ôćĺ* `[Local]`: from `.claude/settings.local.json` 420Ôćĺ 420Ôćĺ* `[Plugin]`: from a plugin's `hooks/hooks.json`, read-only 421Ôćĺ 421Ôćĺ 422Ôćĺ 422Ôćĺ### Disable or remove hooks 423Ôćĺ 423Ôćĺ 424Ôćĺ 424ÔćĺTo remove a hook, delete its entry from the settings JSON file, or use the `/hooks` menu and select the hook to delete it. 425Ôćĺ 425Ôćĺ 426Ôćĺ 426ÔćĺTo temporarily disable all hooks without removing them, set `"disableAllHooks": true` in your settings file or use the toggle in the `/hooks` menu. There is no way to disable an individual hook while keeping it in the configuration. 427Ôćĺ 427Ôćĺ 428Ôćĺ 428ÔćĺThe `disableAllHooks` setting respects the managed settings hierarchy. If an administrator has configured hooks through managed policy settings, `disableAllHooks` set in user, project, or local settings cannot disable those managed hooks. Only `disableAllHooks` set at the managed settings level can disable managed hooks. 429Ôćĺ 429Ôćĺ 430Ôćĺ 430ÔćĺDirect edits to hooks in settings files don't take effect immediately. Claude Code captures a snapshot of hooks at startup and uses it throughout the session. This prevents malicious or accidental hook modifications from taking effect mid-session without your review. If hooks are modified externally, Claude Code warns you and requires review in the `/hooks` menu before changes apply. 431Ôćĺ 431Ôćĺ 432Ôćĺ 432Ôćĺ## Hook input and output 433Ôćĺ 433Ôćĺ 434Ôćĺ 434ÔćĺCommand hooks receive JSON data via stdin and communicate results through exit codes, stdout, and stderr. HTTP hooks receive the same JSON as the POST request body and communicate results through the HTTP response body. This section covers fields and behavior common to all events. Each event's section under [Hook events](#hook-events) includes its specific input schema and decision control options. 435Ôćĺ 435Ôćĺ 436Ôćĺ 436Ôćĺ### Common input fields 437Ôćĺ 437Ôćĺ 438Ôćĺ 438ÔćĺAll hook events receive these fields as JSON, in addition to event-specific fields documented in each [hook event](#hook-events) section. For command hooks, this JSON arrives via stdin. For HTTP hooks, it arrives as the POST request body. 439Ôćĺ 439Ôćĺ 440Ôćĺ 440Ôćĺ| Field | Description | 441Ôćĺ 441Ôćĺ| :---------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 442Ôćĺ 442Ôćĺ| `session_id` | Current session identifier | 443Ôćĺ 443Ôćĺ| `transcript_path` | Path to conversation JSON | 444Ôćĺ 444Ôćĺ| `cwd` | Current working directory when the hook is invoked | 445Ôćĺ 445Ôćĺ| `permission_mode` | Current [permission mode](/en/permissions#permission-modes): `"default"`, `"plan"`, `"acceptEdits"`, `"dontAsk"`, or `"bypassPermissions"` | 446Ôćĺ 446Ôćĺ| `hook_event_name` | Name of the event that fired | 447Ôćĺ 447Ôćĺ 448Ôćĺ 448ÔćĺFor example, a `PreToolUse` hook for a Bash command receives this on stdin: 449Ôćĺ 449Ôćĺ 450Ôćĺ 450Ôćĺ```json theme={null} 451Ôćĺ 451Ôćĺ{ 452Ôćĺ 452Ôćĺ "session_id": "abc123", 453Ôćĺ 453Ôćĺ "transcript_path": "/home/user/.claude/projects/.../transcript.jsonl", 454Ôćĺ 454Ôćĺ "cwd": "/home/user/my-project", 455Ôćĺ 455Ôćĺ "permission_mode": "default", 456Ôćĺ 456Ôćĺ "hook_event_name": "PreToolUse", 457Ôćĺ 457Ôćĺ "tool_name": "Bash", 458Ôćĺ 458Ôćĺ "tool_input": { 459Ôćĺ 459Ôćĺ "command": "npm test" 460Ôćĺ 460Ôćĺ } 461Ôćĺ 461Ôćĺ} 462Ôćĺ 462Ôćĺ``` 463Ôćĺ 463Ôćĺ 464Ôćĺ 464ÔćĺThe `tool_name` and `tool_input` fields are event-specific. Each [hook event](#hook-events) section documents the additional fields for that event. 465Ôćĺ 465Ôćĺ 466Ôćĺ 466Ôćĺ### Exit code output 467Ôćĺ 467Ôćĺ 468Ôćĺ 468ÔćĺThe exit code from your hook command tells Claude Code whether the action should proceed, be blocked, or be ignored. 469Ôćĺ 469Ôćĺ 470Ôćĺ 470Ôćĺ**Exit 0** means success. Claude Code parses stdout for [JSON output fields](#json-output). JSON output is only processed on exit 0. For most events, stdout is only shown in verbose mode (`Ctrl+O`). The exceptions are `UserPromptSubmit` and `SessionStart`, where stdout is added as context that Claude can see and act on. 471Ôćĺ 471Ôćĺ 472Ôćĺ 472Ôćĺ**Exit 2** means a blocking error. Claude Code ignores stdout and any JSON in it. Instead, stderr text is fed back to Claude as an error message. The effect depends on the event: `PreToolUse` blocks the tool call, `UserPromptSubmit` rejects the prompt, and so on. See [exit code 2 behavior](#exit-code-2-behavior-per-event) for the full list. 473Ôćĺ 473Ôćĺ 474Ôćĺ 474Ôćĺ**Any other exit code** is a non-blocking error. stderr is shown in verbose mode (`Ctrl+O`) and execution continues. 475Ôćĺ 475Ôćĺ 476Ôćĺ 476ÔćĺFor example, a hook command script that blocks dangerous Bash commands: 477Ôćĺ 477Ôćĺ 478Ôćĺ 478Ôćĺ```bash theme={null} 479Ôćĺ 479Ôćĺ#!/bin/bash 480Ôćĺ 480Ôćĺ# Reads JSON input from stdin, checks the command 481Ôćĺ 481Ôćĺcommand=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command' < /dev/stdin) 482Ôćĺ 482Ôćĺ 483Ôćĺ 483Ôćĺif [[ "$command" == rm* ]]; then 484Ôćĺ 484Ôćĺ echo "Blocked: rm commands are not allowed" >&2 485Ôćĺ 485Ôćĺ exit 2 # Blocking error: tool call is prevented 486Ôćĺ 486Ôćĺfi 487Ôćĺ 487Ôćĺ 488Ôćĺ 488Ôćĺexit 0 # Success: tool call proceeds 489Ôćĺ 489Ôćĺ``` 490Ôćĺ 490Ôćĺ 491Ôćĺ 491Ôćĺ#### Exit code 2 behavior per event 492Ôćĺ 492Ôćĺ 493Ôćĺ 493ÔćĺExit code 2 is the way a hook signals "stop, don't do this." The effect depends on the event, because some events represent actions that can be blocked (like a tool call that hasn't happened yet) and others represent things that already happened or can't be prevented. 494Ôćĺ 494Ôćĺ 495Ôćĺ 495Ôćĺ| Hook event | Can block? | What happens on exit 2 | 496Ôćĺ 496Ôćĺ| :------------------- | :--------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 497Ôćĺ 497Ôćĺ| `PreToolUse` | Yes | Blocks the tool call | 498Ôćĺ 498Ôćĺ| `PermissionRequest` | Yes | Denies the permission | 499Ôćĺ 499Ôćĺ| `UserPromptSubmit` | Yes | Blocks prompt processing and erases the prompt | 500Ôćĺ 500Ôćĺ| `Stop` | Yes | Prevents Claude from stopping, continues the conversation |

500Ôćĺ   500Ôćĺ| `Stop`               | Yes        | Prevents Claude from stopping, continues the conversation                     |
   501Ôćĺ   501Ôćĺ| `SubagentStop`       | Yes        | Prevents the subagent from stopping                                           |
   502Ôćĺ   502Ôćĺ| `TeammateIdle`       | Yes        | Prevents the teammate from going idle (teammate continues working)            |
   503Ôćĺ   503Ôćĺ| `TaskCompleted`      | Yes        | Prevents the task from being marked as completed                              |
   504Ôćĺ   504Ôćĺ| `ConfigChange`       | Yes        | Blocks the configuration change from taking effect (except `policy_settings`) |
   505Ôćĺ   505Ôćĺ| `PostToolUse`        | No         | Shows stderr to Claude (tool already ran)                                     |
   506Ôćĺ   506Ôćĺ| `PostToolUseFailure` | No         | Shows stderr to Claude (tool already failed)                                  |
   507Ôćĺ   507Ôćĺ| `Notification`       | No         | Shows stderr to user only                                                     |
   508Ôćĺ   508Ôćĺ| `SubagentStart`      | No         | Shows stderr to user only                                                     |
   509Ôćĺ   509Ôćĺ| `SessionStart`       | No         | Shows stderr to user only                                                     |
   510Ôćĺ   510Ôćĺ| `SessionEnd`         | No         | Shows stderr to user only                                                     |
   511Ôćĺ   511Ôćĺ| `PreCompact`         | No         | Shows stderr to user only                                                     |
   512Ôćĺ   512Ôćĺ| `WorktreeCreate`     | Yes        | Any non-zero exit code causes worktree creation to fail                       |
   513Ôćĺ   513Ôćĺ| `WorktreeRemove`     | No         | Failures are logged in debug mode only                                        |
   514Ôćĺ   514Ôćĺ
   515Ôćĺ   515Ôćĺ### HTTP response handling
   516Ôćĺ   516Ôćĺ
   517Ôćĺ   517ÔćĺHTTP hooks use HTTP status codes and response bodies instead of exit codes and stdout:
   518Ôćĺ   518Ôćĺ
   519Ôćĺ   519Ôćĺ* **2xx with an empty body**: success, equivalent to exit code 0 with no output
   520Ôćĺ   520Ôćĺ* **2xx with a plain text body**: success, the text is added as context
   521Ôćĺ   521Ôćĺ* **2xx with a JSON body**: success, parsed using the same [JSON output](#json-output) schema as command hooks
   522Ôćĺ   522Ôćĺ* **Non-2xx status**: non-blocking error, execution continues
   523Ôćĺ   523Ôćĺ* **Connection failure or timeout**: non-blocking error, execution continues
   524Ôćĺ   524Ôćĺ
   525Ôćĺ   525ÔćĺUnlike command hooks, HTTP hooks cannot signal a blocking error through status codes alone. To block a tool call or deny a permission, return a 2xx response with a JSON body containing the appropriate decision fields.
   526Ôćĺ   526Ôćĺ
   527Ôćĺ   527Ôćĺ### JSON output
   528Ôćĺ   528Ôćĺ
   529Ôćĺ   529ÔćĺExit codes let you allow or block, but JSON output gives you finer-grained control. Instead of exiting with code 2 to block, exit 0 and print a JSON object to stdout. Claude Code reads specific fields from that JSON to control behavior, including [decision control](#decision-control) for blocking, allowing, or escalating to the user.
   530Ôćĺ   530Ôćĺ
   531Ôćĺ   531Ôćĺ<Note>
   532Ôćĺ   532Ôćĺ  You must choose one approach per hook, not both: either use exit codes alone for signaling, or exit 0 and print JSON for structured control. Claude Code only processes JSON on exit 0. If you exit 2, any JSON is ignored.
   533Ôćĺ   533Ôćĺ</Note>
   534Ôćĺ   534Ôćĺ
   535Ôćĺ   535ÔćĺYour hook's stdout must contain only the JSON object. If your shell profile prints text on startup, it can interfere with JSON parsing. See [JSON validation failed](/en/hooks-guide#json-validation-failed) in the troubleshooting guide.
   536Ôćĺ   536Ôćĺ
   537Ôćĺ   537ÔćĺThe JSON object supports three kinds of fields:
   538Ôćĺ   538Ôćĺ
   539Ôćĺ   539Ôćĺ* **Universal fields** like `continue` work across all events. These are listed in the table below.
   540Ôćĺ   540Ôćĺ* **Top-level `decision` and `reason`** are used by some events to block or provide feedback.
   541Ôćĺ   541Ôćĺ* **`hookSpecificOutput`** is a nested object for events that need richer control. It requires a `hookEventName` field set to the event name.
   542Ôćĺ   542Ôćĺ
   543Ôćĺ   543Ôćĺ| Field            | Default | Description                                                                                                                |
   544Ôćĺ   544Ôćĺ| :--------------- | :------ | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   545Ôćĺ   545Ôćĺ| `continue`       | `true`  | If `false`, Claude stops processing entirely after the hook runs. Takes precedence over any event-specific decision fields |
   546Ôćĺ   546Ôćĺ| `stopReason`     | none    | Message shown to the user when `continue` is `false`. Not shown to Claude                                                  |
   547Ôćĺ   547Ôćĺ| `suppressOutput` | `false` | If `true`, hides stdout from verbose mode output                                                                           |
   548Ôćĺ   548Ôćĺ| `systemMessage`  | none    | Warning message shown to the user                                                                                          |
   549Ôćĺ   549Ôćĺ
   550Ôćĺ   550ÔćĺTo stop Claude entirely regardless of event type:
   551Ôćĺ   551Ôćĺ
   552Ôćĺ   552Ôćĺ```json  theme={null}
   553Ôćĺ   553Ôćĺ{ "continue": false, "stopReason": "Build failed, fix errors before continuing" }
   554Ôćĺ   554Ôćĺ```
   555Ôćĺ   555Ôćĺ
   556Ôćĺ   556Ôćĺ#### Decision control
   557Ôćĺ   557Ôćĺ
   558Ôćĺ   558ÔćĺNot every event supports blocking or controlling behavior through JSON. The events that do each use a different set of fields to express that decision. Use this table as a quick reference before writing a hook:
   559Ôćĺ   559Ôćĺ
   560Ôćĺ   560Ôćĺ| Events                                                                              | Decision pattern     | Key fields                                                                  |
   561Ôćĺ   561Ôćĺ| :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   562Ôćĺ   562Ôćĺ| UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, Stop, SubagentStop, ConfigChange | Top-level `decision` | `decision: "block"`, `reason`                                               |
   563Ôćĺ   563Ôćĺ| TeammateIdle, TaskCompleted                                                         | Exit code only       | Exit code 2 blocks the action, stderr is fed back as feedback               |
   564Ôćĺ   564Ôćĺ| PreToolUse                                                                          | `hookSpecificOutput` | `permissionDecision` (allow/deny/ask), `permissionDecisionReason`           |
   565Ôćĺ   565Ôćĺ| PermissionRequest                                                                   | `hookSpecificOutput` | `decision.behavior` (allow/deny)                                            |
   566Ôćĺ   566Ôćĺ| WorktreeCreate                                                                      | stdout path          | Hook prints absolute path to created worktree. Non-zero exit fails creation |
   567Ôćĺ   567Ôćĺ| WorktreeRemove, Notification, SessionEnd, PreCompact                                | None                 | No decision control. Used for side effects like logging or cleanup          |
   568Ôćĺ   568Ôćĺ
   569Ôćĺ   569ÔćĺHere are examples of each pattern in action:
   570Ôćĺ   570Ôćĺ
   571Ôćĺ   571Ôćĺ<Tabs>
   572Ôćĺ   572Ôćĺ  <Tab title="Top-level decision">
   573Ôćĺ   573Ôćĺ    Used by `UserPromptSubmit`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `Stop`, `SubagentStop`, and `ConfigChange`. The only value is `"block"`. To allow the action to proceed, omit `decision` from your JSON, or exit 0 without any JSON at all:
   574Ôćĺ   574Ôćĺ
   575Ôćĺ   575Ôćĺ    ```json  theme={null}
   576Ôćĺ   576Ôćĺ    {
   577Ôćĺ   577Ôćĺ      "decision": "block",
   578Ôćĺ   578Ôćĺ      "reason": "Test suite must pass before proceeding"
   579Ôćĺ   579Ôćĺ    }
   580Ôćĺ   580Ôćĺ    ```
   581Ôćĺ   581Ôćĺ  </Tab>
   582Ôćĺ   582Ôćĺ
   583Ôćĺ   583Ôćĺ  <Tab title="PreToolUse">
   584Ôćĺ   584Ôćĺ    Uses `hookSpecificOutput` for richer control: allow, deny, or escalate to the user. You can also modify tool input before it runs or inject additional context for Claude. See [PreToolUse decision control](#pretooluse-decision-control) for the full set of options.
   585Ôćĺ   585Ôćĺ
   586Ôćĺ   586Ôćĺ    ```json  theme={null}
   587Ôćĺ   587Ôćĺ    {
   588Ôćĺ   588Ôćĺ      "hookSpecificOutput": {
   589Ôćĺ   589Ôćĺ        "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
   590Ôćĺ   590Ôćĺ        "permissionDecision": "deny",
   591Ôćĺ   591Ôćĺ        "permissionDecisionReason": "Database writes are not allowed"
   592Ôćĺ   592Ôćĺ      }
   593Ôćĺ   593Ôćĺ    }
   594Ôćĺ   594Ôćĺ    ```
   595Ôćĺ   595Ôćĺ  </Tab>
   596Ôćĺ   596Ôćĺ
   597Ôćĺ   597Ôćĺ  <Tab title="PermissionRequest">
   598Ôćĺ   598Ôćĺ    Uses `hookSpecificOutput` to allow or deny a permission request on behalf of the user. When allowing, you can also modify the tool's input or apply permission rules so the user isn't prompted again. See [PermissionRequest decision control](#permissionrequest-decision-control) for the full set of options.
   599Ôćĺ   599Ôćĺ
   600Ôćĺ   600Ôćĺ    ```json  theme={null}
   601Ôćĺ   601Ôćĺ    {
   602Ôćĺ   602Ôćĺ      "hookSpecificOutput": {
   603Ôćĺ   603Ôćĺ        "hookEventName": "PermissionRequest",
   604Ôćĺ   604Ôćĺ        "decision": {
   605Ôćĺ   605Ôćĺ          "behavior": "allow",
   606Ôćĺ   606Ôćĺ          "updatedInput": {
   607Ôćĺ   607Ôćĺ            "command": "npm run lint"
   608Ôćĺ   608Ôćĺ          }
   609Ôćĺ   609Ôćĺ        }
   610Ôćĺ   610Ôćĺ      }
   611Ôćĺ   611Ôćĺ    }
   612Ôćĺ   612Ôćĺ    ```
   613Ôćĺ   613Ôćĺ  </Tab>
   614Ôćĺ   614Ôćĺ</Tabs>
   615Ôćĺ   615Ôćĺ
   616Ôćĺ   616ÔćĺFor extended examples including Bash command validation, prompt filtering, and auto-approval scripts, see [What you can automate](/en/hooks-guide#what-you-can-automate) in the guide and the [Bash command validator reference implementation](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/examples/hooks/bash_command_validator_example.py).
   617Ôćĺ   617Ôćĺ
   618Ôćĺ   618Ôćĺ## Hook events
   619Ôćĺ   619Ôćĺ
   620Ôćĺ   620ÔćĺEach event corresponds to a point in Claude Code's lifecycle where hooks can run. The sections below are ordered to match the lifecycle: from session setup through the agentic loop to session end. Each section describes when the event fires, what matchers it supports, the JSON input it receives, and how to control behavior through output.
   621Ôćĺ   621Ôćĺ
   622Ôćĺ   622Ôćĺ### SessionStart
   623Ôćĺ   623Ôćĺ
   624Ôćĺ   624ÔćĺRuns when Claude Code starts a new session or resumes an existing session. Useful for loading development context like existing issues or recent changes to your codebase, or setting up environment variables. For static context that does not require a script, use [CLAUDE.md](/en/memory) instead.
   625Ôćĺ   625Ôćĺ
   626Ôćĺ   626ÔćĺSessionStart runs on every session, so keep these hooks fast.
   627Ôćĺ   627Ôćĺ
   628Ôćĺ   628ÔćĺThe matcher value corresponds to how the session was initiated:
   629Ôćĺ   629Ôćĺ
   630Ôćĺ   630Ôćĺ| Matcher   | When it fires                          |
   631Ôćĺ   631Ôćĺ| :-------- | :------------------------------------- |
   632Ôćĺ   632Ôćĺ| `startup` | New session                            |
   633Ôćĺ   633Ôćĺ| `resume`  | `--resume`, `--continue`, or `/resume` |
   634Ôćĺ   634Ôćĺ| `clear`   | `/clear`                               |
   635Ôćĺ   635Ôćĺ| `compact` | Auto or manual compaction              |
   636Ôćĺ   636Ôćĺ
   637Ôćĺ   637Ôćĺ#### SessionStart input
   638Ôćĺ   638Ôćĺ
   639Ôćĺ   639ÔćĺIn addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), SessionStart hooks receive `source`, `model`, and optionally `agent_type`. The `source` field indicates how the session started: `"startup"` for new sessions, `"resume"` for resumed sessions, `"clear"` after `/clear`, or `"compact"` after compaction. The `model` field contains the model identifier. If you start Claude Code with `claude --agent <name>`, an `agent_type` field contains the agent name.
   640Ôćĺ   640Ôćĺ
   641Ôćĺ   641Ôćĺ```json  theme={null}
   642Ôćĺ   642Ôćĺ{
   643Ôćĺ   643Ôćĺ  "session_id": "abc123",
   644Ôćĺ   644Ôćĺ  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
   645Ôćĺ   645Ôćĺ  "cwd": "/Users/...",
   646Ôćĺ   646Ôćĺ  "permission_mode": "default",
   647Ôćĺ   647Ôćĺ  "hook_event_name": "SessionStart",
   648Ôćĺ   648Ôćĺ  "source": "startup",
   649Ôćĺ   649Ôćĺ  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
   650Ôćĺ   650Ôćĺ}
   651Ôćĺ   651Ôćĺ```
   652Ôćĺ   652Ôćĺ
   653Ôćĺ   653Ôćĺ#### SessionStart decision control
   654Ôćĺ   654Ôćĺ
   655Ôćĺ   655ÔćĺAny text your hook script prints to stdout is added as context for Claude. In addition to the [JSON output fields](#json-output) available to all hooks, you can return these event-specific fields:
   656Ôćĺ   656Ôćĺ
   657Ôćĺ   657Ôćĺ| Field               | Description                                                               |
   658Ôćĺ   658Ôćĺ| :------------------ | :------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
   659Ôćĺ   659Ôćĺ| `additionalContext` | String added to Claude's context. Multiple hooks' values are concatenated |
   660Ôćĺ   660Ôćĺ
   661Ôćĺ   661Ôćĺ```json  theme={null}
   662Ôćĺ   662Ôćĺ{
   663Ôćĺ   663Ôćĺ  "hookSpecificOutput": {
   664Ôćĺ   664Ôćĺ    "hookEventName": "SessionStart",
   665Ôćĺ   665Ôćĺ    "additionalContext": "My additional context here"
   666Ôćĺ   666Ôćĺ  }
   667Ôćĺ   667Ôćĺ}
   668Ôćĺ   668Ôćĺ```
   669Ôćĺ   669Ôćĺ
   670Ôćĺ   670Ôćĺ#### Persist environment variables
   671Ôćĺ   671Ôćĺ
   672Ôćĺ   672ÔćĺSessionStart hooks have access to the `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` environment variable, which provides a file path where you can persist environment variables for subsequent Bash commands.
   673Ôćĺ   673Ôćĺ
   674Ôćĺ   674ÔćĺTo set individual environment variables, write `export` statements to `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE`. Use append (`>>`) to preserve variables set by other hooks:
   675Ôćĺ   675Ôćĺ
   676Ôćĺ   676Ôćĺ```bash  theme={null}
   677Ôćĺ   677Ôćĺ#!/bin/bash
   678Ôćĺ   678Ôćĺ
   679Ôćĺ   679Ôćĺif [ -n "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE" ]; then
   680Ôćĺ   680Ôćĺ  echo 'export NODE_ENV=production' >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
   681Ôćĺ   681Ôćĺ  echo 'export DEBUG_LOG=true' >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
   682Ôćĺ   682Ôćĺ  echo 'export PATH="$PATH:./node_modules/.bin"' >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
   683Ôćĺ   683Ôćĺfi
   684Ôćĺ   684Ôćĺ
   685Ôćĺ   685Ôćĺexit 0
   686Ôćĺ   686Ôćĺ```
   687Ôćĺ   687Ôćĺ
   688Ôćĺ   688ÔćĺTo capture all environment changes from setup commands, compare the exported variables before and after:
   689Ôćĺ   689Ôćĺ
   690Ôćĺ   690Ôćĺ```bash  theme={null}
   691Ôćĺ   691Ôćĺ#!/bin/bash
   692Ôćĺ   692Ôćĺ
   693Ôćĺ   693ÔćĺENV_BEFORE=$(export -p | sort)
   694Ôćĺ   694Ôćĺ
   695Ôćĺ   695Ôćĺ# Run your setup commands that modify the environment
   696Ôćĺ   696Ôćĺsource ~/.nvm/nvm.sh
   697Ôćĺ   697Ôćĺnvm use 20
   698Ôćĺ   698Ôćĺ
   699Ôćĺ   699Ôćĺif [ -n "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE" ]; then
   700Ôćĺ   700Ôćĺ  ENV_AFTER=$(export -p | sort)
   701Ôćĺ   701Ôćĺ  comm -13 <(echo "$ENV_BEFORE") <(echo "$ENV_AFTER") >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
   702Ôćĺ   702Ôćĺfi
   703Ôćĺ   703Ôćĺ
   704Ôćĺ   704Ôćĺexit 0
   705Ôćĺ   705Ôćĺ```
   706Ôćĺ   706Ôćĺ
   707Ôćĺ   707ÔćĺAny variables written to this file will be available in all subsequent Bash commands that Claude Code executes during the session.
   708Ôćĺ   708Ôćĺ
   709Ôćĺ   709Ôćĺ<Note>
   710Ôćĺ   710Ôćĺ  `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is available for SessionStart hooks. Other hook types do not have access to this variable.
   711Ôćĺ   711Ôćĺ</Note>
   712Ôćĺ   712Ôćĺ
   713Ôćĺ   713Ôćĺ### UserPromptSubmit
   714Ôćĺ   714Ôćĺ
   715Ôćĺ   715ÔćĺRuns when the user submits a prompt, before Claude processes it. This allows you
   716Ôćĺ   716Ôćĺto add additional context based on the prompt/conversation, validate prompts, or
   717Ôćĺ   717Ôćĺblock certain types of prompts.
   718Ôćĺ   718Ôćĺ
   719Ôćĺ   719Ôćĺ#### UserPromptSubmit input
   720Ôćĺ   720Ôćĺ
   721Ôćĺ   721ÔćĺIn addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), UserPromptSubmit hooks receive the `prompt` field containing the text the user submitted.
   722Ôćĺ   722Ôćĺ
   723Ôćĺ   723Ôćĺ```json  theme={null}
   724Ôćĺ   724Ôćĺ{
   725Ôćĺ   725Ôćĺ  "session_id": "abc123",
   726Ôćĺ   726Ôćĺ  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
   727Ôćĺ   727Ôćĺ  "cwd": "/Users/...",
   728Ôćĺ   728Ôćĺ  "permission_mode": "default",
   729Ôćĺ   729Ôćĺ  "hook_event_name": "UserPromptSubmit",
   730Ôćĺ   730Ôćĺ  "prompt": "Write a function to calculate the factorial of a number"
   731Ôćĺ   731Ôćĺ}
   732Ôćĺ   732Ôćĺ```
   733Ôćĺ   733Ôćĺ
   734Ôćĺ   734Ôćĺ#### UserPromptSubmit decision control
   735Ôćĺ   735Ôćĺ
   736Ôćĺ   736Ôćĺ`UserPromptSubmit` hooks can control whether a user prompt is processed and add context. All [JSON output fields](#json-output) are available.
   737Ôćĺ   737Ôćĺ
   738Ôćĺ   738ÔćĺThere are two ways to add context to the conversation on exit code 0:
   739Ôćĺ   739Ôćĺ
   740Ôćĺ   740Ôćĺ* **Plain text stdout**: any non-JSON text written to stdout is added as context
   741Ôćĺ   741Ôćĺ* **JSON with `additionalContext`**: use the JSON format below for more control. The `additionalContext` field is added as context
   742Ôćĺ   742Ôćĺ
   743Ôćĺ   743ÔćĺPlain stdout is shown as hook output in the transcript. The `additionalContext` field is added more discretely.
   744Ôćĺ   744Ôćĺ
   745Ôćĺ   745ÔćĺTo block a prompt, return a JSON object with `decision` set to `"block"`:
   746Ôćĺ   746Ôćĺ
   747Ôćĺ   747Ôćĺ| Field               | Description                                                                                                        |
   748Ôćĺ   748Ôćĺ| :------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   749Ôćĺ   749Ôćĺ| `decision`          | `"block"` prevents the prompt from being processed and erases it from context. Omit to allow the prompt to proceed |
   750Ôćĺ   750Ôćĺ| `reason`            | Shown to the user when `decision` is `"block"`. Not added to context                                               |
   751Ôćĺ   751Ôćĺ| `additionalContext` | String added to Claude's context                                                                                   |
   752Ôćĺ   752Ôćĺ
   753Ôćĺ   753Ôćĺ```json  theme={null}
   754Ôćĺ   754Ôćĺ{
   755Ôćĺ   755Ôćĺ  "decision": "block",
   756Ôćĺ   756Ôćĺ  "reason": "Explanation for decision",
   757Ôćĺ   757Ôćĺ  "hookSpecificOutput": {
   758Ôćĺ   758Ôćĺ    "hookEventName": "UserPromptSubmit",
   759Ôćĺ   759Ôćĺ    "additionalContext": "My additional context here"
   760Ôćĺ   760Ôćĺ  }
   761Ôćĺ   761Ôćĺ}
   762Ôćĺ   762Ôćĺ```
   763Ôćĺ   763Ôćĺ
   764Ôćĺ   764Ôćĺ<Note>
   765Ôćĺ   765Ôćĺ  The JSON format isn't required for simple use cases. To add context, you can print plain text to stdout with exit code 0. Use JSON when you need to

   769Ôćĺ   769Ôćĺ### PreToolUse

   775Ôćĺ   775Ôćĺ#### PreToolUse input

   872Ôćĺ   872Ôćĺ#### PreToolUse decision control
   873Ôćĺ   873Ôćĺ
   874Ôćĺ   874Ôćĺ`PreToolUse` hooks can control whether a tool call proceeds. Unlike other hooks that use a top-level `decision` field, PreToolUse returns its decision inside a `hookSpecificOutput` object. This gives it richer control: three outcomes (allow, deny, or ask) plus the ability to modify tool input before execution.

Perfect! I have comprehensive documentation. Let me create a summary for you based on all the information I've gathered.

## Claude Code Hooks Documentation - Complete Reference

Based on web search, official documentation, and local project files, here's what I found:

### 1. **Available Hook Events**

These are the main hook events in Claude Code's lifecycle:

| Event | When It Fires | Can Block? |
|-------|---------------|-----------|
| `SessionStart` | Session begins or resumes | No |
| `UserPromptSubmit` | User submits a prompt (before processing) | Yes |
| `PreToolUse` | Before tool execution | Yes |
| `PermissionRequest` | Permission dialog appears | Yes |
| `PostToolUse` | After tool succeeds | No (info only) |
| `PostToolUseFailure` | After tool fails | No (info only) |
| `Notification` | Claude sends a notification | No |
| `SubagentStart` | Subagent spawned | No |
| `SubagentStop` | Subagent finishes | Yes |
| `Stop` | Claude finishes responding | Yes |
| `TeammateIdle` | Agent team member goes idle | Yes |
| `TaskCompleted` | Task marked complete | Yes |
| `ConfigChange` | Config file changes during session | Yes |
| `PreCompact` | Before context compaction | No |
| `WorktreeCreate` | Worktree created | Yes |
| `WorktreeRemove` | Worktree removed | No |
| `SessionEnd` | Session terminates | No |

**Key finding for your auto-sync goal:** `UserPromptSubmit` fires after every prompt submission and can run hooks synchronously.

### 2. **Environment Variables Passed to Hooks**

All hook scripts receive JSON via stdin with these **common fields**:

```json
{
  "session_id": "unique identifier",
  "transcript_path": "/path/to/conversation.jsonl",
  "cwd": "current working directory",
  "permission_mode": "default|plan|acceptEdits|dontAsk|bypassPermissions",
  "hook_event_name": "EventName",
  // Event-specific fields follow
}
```

**Hook environment variables available:**
- `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` - project root (for referencing scripts)
- `CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT` - plugin root (for plugin scripts)
- `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` - environment file for SessionStart hooks only (to persist vars)
- `CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE` - set to "true" in remote web environments

### 3. **Hook Configuration in settings.json**

Hooks are defined in JSON settings at three scope levels:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "EventName": [
      {
        "matcher": "regex|ToolName|*",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command|http|prompt|agent",
            "command": "script path",
            "timeout": 600,
            "statusMessage": "Running...",
            "async": false
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

**Hook locations** (in order of precedence):
- `~/.claude/settings.json` (user - all projects)
- `.claude/settings.json` (project - committed)
- `.claude/settings.local.json` (project - local only)
- `Plugin/hooks/hooks.json` (when plugin enabled)
- Skill/Agent frontmatter (component-scoped)
- Managed policy settings (organization-wide)

**Reference scripts** using environment variables:
```json
{ "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/script.sh" }
{ "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/script.sh" }
```

### 4. **Hook Script Best Practices**

**Exit Codes:**
- `exit 0` - Success. Script can output JSON for structured control.
- `exit 2` - Blocking error. stderr is shown as error message.
- Any other code - Non-blocking error. stderr shown in verbose mode.

**JSON Output Schema** (print to stdout on exit 0):

```json
{
  "continue": true|false,
  "stopReason": "message when continue=false",
  "systemMessage": "warning shown to user",
  "suppressOutput": false,
  "decision": "block",
  "reason": "explanation",
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "EventName",
    "permissionDecision": "allow|deny|ask",
    "permissionDecisionReason": "reason",
    "updatedInput": { "field": "value" },
    "additionalContext": "text for claude"
  }
}
```

**Key patterns:**

- **Command hooks** - receive JSON on stdin, communicate via exit code + stdout
- **HTTP hooks** - POST JSON body, response body uses same format
- **Prompt hooks** - send prompt to LLM for yes/no decision
- **Agent hooks** - spawn subagent with tools (Read, Grep, Glob, etc.)

**stdout/stderr handling:**
- `exit 0 + JSON`: structured control
- `exit 0 + plain text`: added as context (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit)
- `exit 2 + stderr`: blocks action, stderr is error message
- `exit other + stderr`: non-blocking error (verbose mode only)

### 5. **Filesystem and Event Coverage**

**Critical limitation for auto-sync:** There are **NO file-watch or filesystem-change events**. All hooks are prompt-based:

- Session lifecycle: SessionStart, SessionEnd, PreCompact
- Prompt handling: UserPromptSubmit
- Tool-based: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, PermissionRequest
- Agent-based: SubagentStart, SubagentStop
- Config-based: ConfigChange
- Special: WorktreeCreate, WorktreeRemove

**For your auto-sync use case:**
- `UserPromptSubmit` is your primary trigger (runs after every prompt)
- `PostToolUse` with matcher on tool names (Bash, Write, Edit)
- `SessionEnd` for final cleanup/commit
- No real-time file watching - only event-driven hooks

### 6. **Real-World Example from Your Repo**

Your existing `rtk-rewrite.sh` hook demonstrates the pattern:

```json
{
  "PreToolUse": [
    {
      "matcher": "Bash",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "command",
          "command": "bash rtk-rewrite.sh",
          "timeout": 5
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

The script can return:
```json
{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
    "permissionDecision": "allow",
    "updatedInput": { "command": "rtk git log" }
  }
}
```

This transparently rewrites the command before execution.
